metric_value: 75 face-to-face and telephone interviews with storage managers at companies with $1B+ revenue; includes several Fortune 50-size businesses
notes: US-focused; financial services over-represented; government/non-profits under-represented; conducted Q2 2003

metric_value: Storage accessed by servers using: mainframe; Windows; Unix/Linux
notes: Three OS categories covered ensuring cross-platform applicability of findings

metric_value: More than half of enterprises are completely run online using Fibre Channel (FC) and SCSI disks
notes: Establishes the incumbent storage baseline that midline is designed to disrupt

metric_value: ATA disks have 2-8x storage capacity of FC/SCSI; up to 300 GB per ATA disk compared to 142/72/36 GB per FC/SCSI disk
notes: Fundamental economic advantage driving midline adoption

metric_name: ATA vs FC/SCSI cost per GB advantage
metric_value: ATA disks are about half the cost per gigabyte versus FC/SCSI
notes: Cost-per-GB parity/advantage is central economic driver for midline tier

metric_value: SCSI disks can reach more than 5x the cost of ATA disks
notes: Cost multiple validates economic case for midline tier

metric_name: Midline system cost savings vs high-performance disk
metric_value: Midline solutions can be as much as half the price of high-performance disk-based systems
notes: System-level cost savings beyond just disk unit cost

metric_value: ATA-based: $20K-$30K vs SCSI-based: $35K-$50K
notes: Concrete cost comparison for 2TB disk-based backup appliance favoring ATA

metric_name: Large FC environment savings from midline migration
metric_value: In 30TB+ FC environments: savings greater than $100K off deals formerly $300K+
notes: Compelling ROI for large enterprise FC environments migrating fixed-content to midline

metric_value: Over 25% of enterprise storage buyers showed high willingness to purchase higher-capacity/lower-cost disks
notes: Primary research finding; high-willingness segment

metric_value: Nearly 50% showed moderate willingness to purchase higher-capacity/lower-cost disks
notes: Combined with high willingness: ~75% moderately or highly likely to buy midline storage

metric_name: Midline purchase intent — combined likely buyers
metric_value: 75% of storage buyers are moderately or highly likely to buy mid-line storage in the coming year
notes: KEY FINDING: headline statistic for the white paper; validated market demand

metric_name: Highly likely to purchase low-cost disk storage
metric_value: ~40% of respondents said highly likely to purchase low-cost disk storage
notes: Strong leading-edge segment

metric_name: Current supplier discussion of low-cost options
metric_value: 60% of respondents said their current storage supplier had already discussed low-cost storage options with them
notes: Market is already primed — suppliers already sensing demand

metric_value: 60% of respondents have single applications with very large non-changing/static data (video images data warehouse detailed data old e-mails)
notes: Identifies the primary use case for midline: large static data workloads

metric_value: Nearly 2/3 (67%) of respondents said restoring critical data takes longer than desired
notes: Major pain point driving midline adoption as a tape-replacement for near-term recovery

metric_value: Over 60% of survey respondents report data restorations more than once a year; 20% report a data restore monthly or more frequently
notes: High restore frequency amplifies the pain of slow tape-based restoration

metric_value: ~50% of respondents said backup management is a burden
notes: Secondary pain point supporting midline backup appliance use case

metric_name: Critical data restoration guarantee failure rate
metric_value: ~50% of respondents would not guarantee all critical data can be restored on a given day
notes: Alarming reliability gap: half of enterprises cannot guarantee restoration — major impetus for midline disk-to-disk backup

metric_value: >80% of survey respondents said backup windows could be a problem with off-line backups
notes: Critical operational constraint; midline disk-based backup addresses shrinking backup windows

metric_value: Ageing (value and use of content change as content ages); Freezing (content changes from dynamic to fixed); Accumulation (data grows; access per piece drops); Redundancy (more copies for faster access and data protection)
notes: Four principles of ILM used to justify midline tier placement; seminal framework definition

metric_value: Level 1 (top): Solid-state disks (ultra-performance); Level 2: High-performance FC/SCSI (mission-critical OLTP); Level 3: Mid-line ATA (fixed content aged data); Level 4 (bottom): Tape (backup/restore off-site)
notes: Figure 1 in white paper: The Storage Pyramid — Four Key Levels; Source: Aberdeen Group June 2003

metric_value: High-capacity online ATA disks in the middle tier between high-use FC/SCSI disk arrays and magnetic tape off-line storage; aimed at data that needs to be online with moderate access and rate-of-change requirements
notes: Authoritative definition of the new storage tier

metric_name: Data migration to midline tier — minimum estimate
metric_value: At least 20% of enterprise data fits the definition of inactive/seldom-used online storage appropriate for high-capacity/low-cost ATA
notes: Conservative floor estimate for midline addressable data

metric_name: Data migration to midline tier — long-term projection
metric_value: Over half of enterprise data will eventually fall into the mid-line
notes: PRESCIENCE HIGH: Wikibon 2011 estimated SATA holds 85% of enterprise data by 2015 — forecast proved conservative

metric_value: Wikibon 2011 estimated SATA high-capacity disks would hold 85% of enterprise data and 40% of storage spend by 2015
notes: Actual outcome: exceeds the >50% prediction; confirms and strengthens OBS-025

metric_name: Traditional OLTP — stays on high-performance disk
metric_value: Traditional OLTP (order entry): update-intensive; keep on high-performance disk; closed transactions (shipped orders) may move to midline
notes: Application classification for storage tier guidance

metric_name: Mixed OLTP applications — partial midline viability
metric_value: Contemporary mixed applications (e.g. SAP/R3): mix of writes and reads; if data can be segregated mid-line may work for static portions
notes: Data segregation within applications enables partial midline adoption

metric_name: Business Intelligence and Data Warehousing — strong midline fit
metric_value: Query-intensive (sequential reads); mid-line storage can play a significant role; compelling cost-per-GB argument
notes: BI/DW identified as primary enterprise use case for midline; large detail tables specifically called out

metric_name: Fixed large file workloads — strong midline fit
metric_value: X-rays MRIs: fixed after creation; low access frequency; midline sufficient. Large distribution files (MP3 video): read-only copies; midline or high-perf depending on performance requirements
notes: Medical imaging and media content as clear midline use cases

metric_name: ATA reliability concern addressed via RAID
metric_value: ATA disk reliability has evolved to high level; individual disks protected as RAID group; higher capacity drives means fewer drives that can fail
notes: Addresses enterprise administrator skepticism about ATA reliability in enterprise environments

metric_value: 1. Embedded in array or storage appliance (incremental case-by-case); 2. Proactive architectural planning (strategic storage pyramid review)
notes: Two-path adoption framework; strategy #2 preferred for maximum cost savings

metric_value: IT must adopt QoS perspective; not all applications require same service levels; create formal or informal SLAs; long-term: storage utility as part of information utility
notes: Organizational/governance change required to capture midline cost savings

metric_value: Enterprises that manage ILM effectively will: cut disk storage costs; balance performance to need; focus IT administrator resources; achieve greater scalability and flexibility
notes: Four-outcome framework for ILM benefit realization

metric_name: Midline storage characterization as quiet revolution
metric_value: Mid-line storage is a 'quiet' storage revolution; enterprises are still learning how to best use the new tier
notes: Author's characterization: transformative but not yet recognized as such — 2003 adoption still early

metric_name: Midline storage as critical component of every enterprise
metric_value: Time and technology have arrived for mid-line storage to become a critical and cost-effective component of every enterprise storage strategy
notes: PRESCIENCE HIGH: Proved correct — tiered storage (midline tier) is now standard in every enterprise storage product

metric_name: Tiered storage as universal enterprise architecture
metric_value: By 2006 Warner Bros running multi-tiered FC/SATA/tape environment; tiered storage became standard feature of every enterprise storage product (EMC NetApp Dell etc.)
notes: Actual outcome confirming OBS-036 prediction

metric_name: ILM aging pattern — data in middle age
metric_value: Most data is in middle age where value is less and where mid-line disk is most appropriate
notes: Key insight: majority of enterprise data by volume is in the 'middle-age' lifecycle stage — validates midline tier's addressable market

metric_value: Nearline (Disk Backup and Staging) and Offline (Tape Archive) tiers named as part of four-level storage hierarchy
notes: PRESCIENCE HIGH: 'Nearline' is still used as standard enterprise storage terminology today

metric_value: Aberdeen's 4 Pools lexicon (Online Midline Nearline Offline) became industry standard terminology; 'nearline' storage remains standard enterprise storage vocabulary
notes: Actual outcome: lexicon creation succeeded; 'nearline' in particular persists in enterprise storage discussions
